title: Workbook — God is Father
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class or in a group. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Section 1 — Remembering the Essentials

Review the key points from the lesson and answer each question.

  1. What name did God reveal to Moses at the burning bush? What does that name mean?
  2. The word «Abba» that Jesus used—what does it literally mean, and why was it so radical?
  3. Where in the Bible does it say «God is love»? (Give the full reference.)
  4. Who is the true protagonist of the parable of the Prodigal Son?
  5. According to St. Paul, what spirit did we receive: a spirit of slavery or a spirit of adoption?
  6. What does St. Thérèse of Lisieux's «Little Way» teach us about trusting God as Father?

Section 2 — For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question honestly. There are no «correct» answers to these personal questions.

1. Describe a moment when you experienced God's love as that of a father (or mother). How did you feel?

Take a moment to remember. If nothing comes to mind, think of an experience of unconditional love you received from someone.

 

 

 

2. What sometimes prevents you from relating to God with the trust of a child?

Is it fear? Guilt? The feeling that God is distant or angry? Something from your past?

 

 

 

3. If you knew with absolute certainty that God loves you unconditionally, what would you change in your life?

What would you do differently? What would you fear less? What weight would you set down?

 

 

 

4. The parable of the Prodigal Son has two sons. Which one do you identify with more today? Why?

Are you the one who left and needs to return? Or the one who stayed but is resentful? Perhaps a mix of both?

 

 

 

5. How can you live this week with greater filial trust toward God?

Name one concrete action: a prayer, a decision, a conversation you need to have.

 

 

 

Section 3 — Practical Activities

A. Read Luke 15:11-32 Aloud

Read it first from the younger son's perspective. Then read it again putting yourself in the older son's shoes. What feelings arise? Write a brief reflection on the experience.

 

 

 

 

B. Pray the Our Father Slowly

Take each phrase of the Our Father and pause after each one. Write down what each phrase tells you about who God is for you, and who you are in relation to him.

Our Father, who art in heaven... —

 

Hallowed be thy name... —

 

Thy kingdom come... —

 

Thy will be done... —

 

Give us this day our daily bread... —

 

Forgive us our trespasses... —

 

Lead us not into temptation... —

 

Deliver us from evil... —

 

C. Write a Letter to God as Father

Begin with «Dear Father God...» and write what is on your heart: your joys, your fears, your questions, your gratitude. Don't worry about making it sound beautiful; just be honest. This letter is for you and God—no one else needs to read it unless you choose to share it.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

D. Reflect on Isaiah 49:15

Read this verse aloud: «Can a woman forget her nursing child, or show no compassion for the child of her womb? Even if she might forget, yet I will not forget you.» Sit quietly for two minutes. What does it mean that God's love exceeds even a mother's love? What part of you needs to hear this?

 

 

 

 

E. Look at Rembrandt's «The Return of the Prodigal Son»

Find an image of this painting online or in a book. Spend at least five minutes simply looking at it. Notice the father's hands (one masculine, one feminine), the son's bare foot, the light, the faces of the onlookers. What details speak to you? Write down your observations.

 

 

 

 

F. Find God as Father in a Psalm

Read Psalm 103:8-14 slowly. Underline or highlight every word that describes God's character. Then rewrite verses 13-14 in your own words, as if you were explaining to a friend what it means that God «knows our frame, he remembers that we are dust.»

 

 

 

 

Section 4 — Memory Verse

«For you did not receive a spirit of slavery to fall back into fear, but you received a spirit of adoption, through which we cry, 'Abba, Father!'»

— Romans 8:15

Bonus verse: «See what love the Father has given us, that we should be called children of God; and so we are.» (1 John 3:1)

Section 5 — Prayer for This Week

Our Father, who art in heaven,
hallowed be thy name.
Thank you for being a Father who runs toward me,
who embraces me before I speak,
who doesn't ask for explanations,
who celebrates when I return.
Help me to trust in your love,
to live as a child, not a slave,
and to forgive as you forgive.
Teach me to see others as your children too,
so that I never treat a stranger
like anything less than family.
And when I suffer, when I don't understand,
hold me close and remind me:
I am your child, and nothing can snatch me
out of your hands.
Amen.

Section 6 — Going Deeper (Optional)

If you want to explore this topic further, here are suggestions:

  • Read Chapter 1 of Henri Nouwen's The Return of the Prodigal Son. Notice how he connects his own life to the characters in the painting.
  • Read the first five chapters of St. Thérèse's Story of a Soul. Pay attention to how she speaks about God the Father.
  • Find an image of Michelangelo's «The Creation of Adam» from the Sistine Chapel. Meditate on the space between God's finger and Adam's. What does that space represent in your own life?
  • In the Catechism, read paragraphs 198-231 (God the Father) and 2779-2785 (the Our Father). Underline anything that surprises you or that you want to remember.
Print Workbook
